narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, including your middle family income bracket and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 67.4% chance that you would fail it, tipping the decision toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor bar passage outcomes for the school. This prediction hinged most strongly on your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school, specifically your position in the 2nd decile for both Year 1 and Year 3 grades—far below the average 5th or 6th decile spots for most students. Your Year 3 decile ranking had the single biggest pull toward the failure prediction, as being in the lower ranks signaled to the model a pattern of underperformance relative to peers that often correlates with bar struggles. Similarly, your Year 1 decile exerted a strong comparable influence in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about foundational academic habits. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA pushed meaningfully against the failure prediction, helping to offset some of those earlier signals and showing the model your potential for stronger performance in college-level work. Compared to typical students, where LSAT scores average around 36.7 and yours is 31, that factor played a supporting role but wasn't the top driver here—your high school deciles overshadowed it. While your background as a full-time aspiring lawyer from a middle-income family aligns with the majority, it couldn't counterbalance the dominant weight of those low decile rankings in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your character or drive, but a data-driven assessment based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, gathering stronger reference letters that highlight your undergraduate strengths, or even pursuing additional coursework to demonstrate improved academic deciles. You might also appeal the decision with evidence of extenuating circumstances from those early school years, or target schools with more holistic reviews that weigh your GPA more heavily. With targeted improvements, especially in showcasing upward trends from your undergrad success, you can reshape how models like this one see your bar exam potential next time.
